one_line_takeaway: Discrete image tokenizers used as image encoders in multimodal foundation models are highly vulnerable to cheap, label-free adversarial attacks (0% robust accuracy in several settings) that can force targeted unsafe LLM outputs, but unsupervised adversarial fine-tuning of only the tokenizer's encoder restores substantial, well-generalizing robustness at 2.2x lower training cost than full end-to-end adversarial training.
key_finding: Original (non-robustified) tokenizers give essentially zero robust accuracy under end-to-end attack: e.g., FuseLIP-B clean tokenizer drops from 93.6% clean to 0.0% robust accuracy at eps=4/255 on Imagenette, and zero-shot UniTok drops from 99.2% clean to 0.0% at eps=2/255 on Imagenette (Table 2); UniTok-MLLM VQA average accuracy falls from 66.9% clean to 6.4% at eps=4/255 (Table 3). Unsupervised embedding-space attacks (label-free, tokenizer-only) achieve robust accuracy close to full end-to-end supervised attacks in classification (Fig. 1), despite requiring no downstream access. The proposed unsupervised adversarial fine-tuning substantially restores robustness while incurring an explicit accuracy/robustness trade-off controlled by training radius: e.g., FuseLIP average robust accuracy rises from 0.6% (clean tokenizer, eps=4/255) to 27.0% (AT eps=4/255) up to 40.6% (AT eps=16/255), with corresponding clean accuracy dropping from 77.3% to 74.6% down to 63.5% (Table 1). For UniTok-MLLM VQA, robust tokenizers raise average accuracy under eps=4/255 attack from 6.4% (clean tokenizer) to 35.2-40.0% across AT radii (Table 3), while clean accuracy declines modestly from 66.9% to 54.6-62.5%. Robustness transfers well beyond the ImageNet-1k fine-tuning distribution to OpenImages retrieval, VQA, and captioning tasks. Compared to full end-to-end supervised adversarial fine-tuning of a downstream classifier, the tokenizer-only unsupervised approach generalizes far better to unseen tasks/datasets (Table 4: end-to-end AT collapses OI-Pos/OI-Crop clean accuracy to 31.8%/15.2% vs. 58.0%/60.4% for unsupervised AT on ImageNet), while being 2.2x faster per training step (1.17s vs. 2.56s per sample) because it back-propagates only through the 25.8M-parameter encoder rather than the full 68M-parameter system. Unsuccessful attacks on clean tokenizers still flip most token indices (avg. 125-126 of 128 for FuseLIP-S/B), indicating index-level changes alone are not what drives attack success, and motivating the paper's choice to attack pre-quantization embeddings rather than discrete indices. Targeted captioning attacks succeed in eliciting unsafe/policy-violating captions (fraud, harassment) from the original UniTok-MLLM at low perturbation strength (eps=4/255), while the robust tokenizer preserves safe, accurate captions under the same attacks.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of adversarial robustness for discrete image tokenizers (e.g., TiTok, FlexTok, UniTok), which are vector-quantization-based image encoders increasingly used in multimodal encoder-decoder and decoder-only foundation models as an alternative to CLIP/DINO. The authors (1) propose an unsupervised, task-agnostic embedding-space attack that perturbs an image to maximize the l2 distance between pre-quantization embeddings of clean and perturbed inputs (Eq. 1), requiring no labels or access to downstream components, and show it is often competitive with end-to-end supervised attacks (e.g., AutoAttack, APGD-CE) that need full model/task access; and (2) propose an unsupervised adversarial fine-tuning defense (Eq. 2, extending Schlarmann et al. 2024's RobustCLIP approach to discrete tokenizers) that fine-tunes only the tokenizer's encoder (keeping codebook and downstream components frozen) to make embeddings invariant to on-the-fly adversarial perturbations, using unlabeled images.
threat_model: Two attacker capability levels are studied: (a) unsupervised/tokenizer-only access -- the attacker perturbs the input image and can query only the discrete tokenizer's pre-quantization embeddings, without labels or downstream task/model access (e.g., cannot see the LLM in an MLLM pipeline); (b) end-to-end/supervised access -- the attacker has full access to the downstream model (classifier, VQA model, captioning MLLM) and optimizes task-specific losses (cross-entropy, targeted caption log-likelihood) via APGD/AutoAttack, using a straight-through estimator to back-propagate through the non-differentiable quantization step. Perturbations are l_inf-bounded with radii in {2,4,8,12,16}/255. A concrete real-world-motivated attack scenario is demonstrated: a targeted image perturbation causing UniTok-MLLM to output attacker-chosen unsafe captions (e.g., a fraudulent bank-transfer request or harassment), showing the tokenizer vulnerability translates into a controllable, policy-violating LLM output without needing white-box access to the LLM itself.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to three tokenizer families (TiTok, FlexTok, UniTok) and a specific set of downstream models (FuseLIP, UniTok-MLLM); FlexTok is only evaluated in isolation (not plugged into a larger downstream system). The robustness-clean-accuracy trade-off is explicit and non-trivial (e.g., FuseLIP clean accuracy loss of up to ~14 points at the highest training radius), and the authors note training at eps=16/255 for UniTok is 'excessive,' underperforming eps=12/255 on both clean and robust accuracy. The defense is only applied to the tokenizer's encoder; codebook and downstream components remain frozen and unexamined as potential attack surfaces. The paper explicitly flags as future work how tokenizer design choices (VQ vs. FSQ, codebook size, feature dimension) affect robustness, and notes structural differences across tokenizers in reconstruction distortion (TiTok most distorted, FlexTok most robust) that are not mechanistically explained.

datasets_used: DataComp (UniTok pretraining, cited not run by authors)
quantitative_results: Fig.1: unsupervised attacks perform close to end-to-end supervised attacks across TiTok/FlexTok/UniTok classifiers on Imagenette, both with 100 APGD iterations on 500 images
Table 1 (FuseLIP avg over 4 datasets): clean tokenizer 77.3% clean / 4.0% (eps=2/255) / 0.6% (eps=4/255); AT eps=4/255: 74.6%/52.1%/27.0%; AT eps=16/255: 63.5%/52.6%/40.6%
Table 2 (UniTok avg over Imagenette/Caltech101/ImageNet): clean tokenizer 84.1%/0.0%/0.0%; AT eps=4/255: 82.4%/60.3%/36.0%; AT eps=16/255: 66.7%/58.7%/49.3%
Table 3 (UniTok-MLLM VQA avg over VQAv2/OK-VQA/GQA): clean tokenizer 66.9%/10.0%/6.4%; AT eps=4/255: 62.5%/45.2%/35.2%; AT eps=8/255: 59.1%/47.1%/39.4% (best average at eps=8-12/255)

Table 4: end-to-end AT on Imagenette reaches 90.6% clean/79.4% robust on the training task but collapses to 31.8%/21.6% (OI-Pos) and 15.2%/9.6% (OI-Crop); unsupervised AT (ImageNet) gives 84.2%/64.4% (Imagenette), 58.0%/48.0% (OI-Pos), 60.4%/42.2% (OI-Crop)
Runtime: unsupervised tokenizer-only AT step = 1.17s/sample vs. supervised end-to-end AT step = 2.56s/sample (2.2x reduction), backpropagating through 25.8M vs. 68M parameters
Token-index analysis: even unsuccessful attacks on clean tokenizers flip an average of 125-126 of 128 token indices for FuseLIP-S/B
baselines_compared: end-to-end supervised adversarial attacks (APGD cross-entropy, AutoAttack)
end-to-end supervised adversarial fine-tuning of the full model (encoder+codebook+classifier)
original/clean (non-fine-tuned) tokenizers as the no-defense baseline
four alternative attack objective formulations (pre/post-quantization combinations, App. B.3, Table 9)
